Question.content: Which element has the lowest boiling point?

Answer.content: Helium

Explanation.content:
Lowest Boiling Point Element

The Answer: Helium
Helium (He) has the lowest boiling point of any element at 4.2 K (-268.95°C).
Why is helium's boiling point so low?
  • It is a noble gas with a completely filled electron shell (1s²)
  • It has extremely weak interatomic forces (London dispersion forces) due to its small size and low number of electrons
  • It has high zero-point energy (quantum mechanical effect) that keeps atoms in motion even at extremely low temperatures
Interesting Fact:
Helium is the only element that cannot be solidified by cooling alone at normal atmospheric pressure - it remains liquid down to absolute zero. To form solid helium, pressure must be applied (about 25 atmospheres).
✓ Final Answer: Helium (He) has the lowest boiling point of any element.

Question.content: What are the two metallic forms of oxygen?

Answer.content: Ozone (O₃) and Dioxygen (O₂) – the two common allotropes of oxygen

Explanation.content:
Forms of Oxygen

Understanding Allotropes:
Allotropes are different structural forms of the same element in the same physical state.
The Two Main Allotropes of Oxygen:
1. Dioxygen (O₂):
  • The common form of oxygen we breathe
  • Makes up about 21% of Earth's atmosphere
  • Colorless, odorless gas at room temperature
  • Essential for respiration in most living organisms
2. Ozone (O₃):
  • A pale blue gas with a distinct pungent odor
  • Found in the stratosphere (ozone layer)
  • Less stable than O₂ and decomposes to O₂
  • Forms a protective layer that absorbs harmful UV radiation
Note on "Metallic Forms":
Under extremely high pressure, solid oxygen can become metallic (ε-phase or ζ-phase), exhibiting metallic conductivity. However, the question likely refers to the two common allotropes: O₂ and O₃.
✓ Final Answer: The two forms of oxygen are dioxygen (O₂) and ozone (O₃).

Question.content: Fluorine brings out the highest oxidation state of other elements as a result of its what two properties?

Answer.content: Small size and high electronegativity

Explanation.content:
Fluorine's Unique Properties

Why Fluorine is Special:
Fluorine is the most reactive non-metal and has two key properties that allow it to bring out the highest oxidation states in other elements:
1. Small Size:
  • Fluorine has the smallest atomic radius among halogens (only 71 pm)
  • Its small size allows it to approach other atoms closely and form strong bonds
  • It can fit into crowded coordination spheres around central atoms
2. High Electronegativity:
  • Fluorine is the most electronegative element (Pauling electronegativity = 3.98)
  • It strongly pulls electrons away from other atoms
  • This oxidizes the other element to its highest possible oxidation state
Examples of Highest Oxidation States Achieved by Fluorine:
  • IF₇ - Iodine reaches +7 oxidation state (highest for iodine)
  • SF₆ - Sulfur reaches +6 oxidation state
  • PtF₆ - Platinum reaches +6 oxidation state
  • ClF₃, ClF₅ - Chlorine reaches +3 and +5 oxidation states
✓ Final Answer: Fluorine brings out the highest oxidation state of other elements due to its small size and high electronegativity.

Question.content: A group (V) element is a white solid with strong attraction for water and it reacts with water to form what products?

Answer.content: Phosphorus (P₄) reacts with water to form phosphine (PH₃) and phosphorous acid (H₃PO₃)

Explanation.content:
White Phosphorus Reaction with Water

Identifying the Element:
The group V element described is white phosphorus (P₄). It is:
  • A white or yellowish waxy solid
  • Highly reactive and pyrophoric (ignites in air)
  • Has a strong affinity for water (hygroscopic)
  • Stored under water to prevent contact with air
Reaction with Water:
White phosphorus reacts slowly with water, especially in the presence of light, to produce:
\(P_4 + 6H_2O \rightarrow PH_3 + 3H_3PO_3\)
  • PH₃ (Phosphine): A toxic, flammable gas with a garlic-like odor
  • H₃PO₃ (Phosphorous acid): A weak acid that remains in solution
Note:
This reaction is slow at room temperature but accelerates with heating. The phosphine produced can sometimes ignite spontaneously in air, giving rise to "will-o'-the-wisps" phenomenon.
✓ Final Answer: White phosphorus (P₄) reacts with water to form phosphine (PH₃) and phosphorous acid (H₃PO₃).

Question.content: The ability of group (III) elements to withhold its s-electrons from participating in bonding is known as what?

Answer.content: Inert pair effect

Explanation.content:
The Inert Pair Effect

Definition:
The inert pair effect is the tendency of the two electrons in the outermost s-orbital (ns²) of heavier p-block elements to remain non-ionized or non-bonding.
Why Does It Happen?
  • As we move down a group, the ns² electrons penetrate closer to the nucleus
  • They are held more tightly due to poor shielding by intervening d and f electrons
  • These s-electrons experience higher effective nuclear charge
  • They become 'inert' - less available for bonding or ionization
Effect on Group III Elements (Boron Family):
Element Common Oxidation States Trend
B, Al Only +3 No inert pair effect
Ga, In +3 and some +1 Inert pair effect appears
Tl (Thallium) Predominantly +1 Strong inert pair effect
✓ Final Answer: The phenomenon is called the inert pair effect.

Question.content: Nitrogen Sesquioxide is an unstable acidic oxide of Nitrogen which can be obtained by reacting what with what?

Answer.content: Nitrogen monoxide (NO) with nitrogen dioxide (NO₂)

Explanation.content:
Nitrogen Sesquioxide (N₂O₃)

What is Nitrogen Sesquioxide?
Nitrogen sesquioxide is dinitrogen trioxide (N₂O₃). It is:
  • An unstable blue liquid at low temperatures
  • The anhydride of nitrous acid (HNO₂)
  • An acidic oxide that reacts with water to form nitrous acid
Preparation Method:
N₂O₃ is obtained by mixing equimolar amounts of nitrogen monoxide (NO) and nitrogen dioxide (NO₂) at low temperatures:
\(NO + NO_2 \rightleftharpoons N_2O_3\)

The reaction is reversible and favors N₂O₃ formation only at low temperatures (below -20°C). At room temperature, N₂O₃ dissociates back into NO and NO₂.

Properties:
  • Deep blue color in liquid and solid states
  • Unstable and dissociates easily
  • Reacts with water: \(N_2O_3 + H_2O \rightarrow 2HNO_2\) (nitrous acid)
  • Reacts with alkalis to form nitrites
✓ Final Answer: Nitrogen sesquioxide (N₂O₃) is obtained by reacting nitrogen monoxide (NO) with nitrogen dioxide (NO₂).

Question.content: Nitrogen cannot extend its covalency to five due to what two reasons?

Answer.content: Absence of d-orbitals and small size

Explanation.content:
Why Nitrogen's Covalency is Limited to 4

Understanding Covalency:
Covalency is the number of covalent bonds an atom can form. While phosphorus (below nitrogen in Group 15) can form up to 5 covalent bonds (e.g., PCl₅), nitrogen cannot exceed 4.
Reason 1: Absence of d-orbitals
  • Nitrogen is in Period 2, so its valence shell is n=2
  • The second shell has only s and p orbitals (2s and 2p) - no d-orbitals
  • To expand its octet beyond 8 electrons, an atom needs available d-orbitals to accommodate extra electrons
  • Nitrogen simply doesn't have any d-orbitals to use
Reason 2: Small Size
  • Nitrogen has a very small atomic radius (about 75 pm)
  • Its small size cannot physically accommodate five large atoms or groups around it
  • There isn't enough space for five bonds due to steric hindrance
Comparison with Phosphorus:
Phosphorus (Period 3) has:
  • Available 3d orbitals to expand its octet
  • Larger size to accommodate more atoms
  • Can form PCl₅ (covalency 5) and PF₆⁻ (covalency 6)
✓ Final Answer: Nitrogen cannot extend its covalency to five due to the absence of d-orbitals and its small size.

Question.content: How many degenerate orbitals are in energy level n=4?

Explanation.content:
Degenerate Orbitals in Energy Level n=4

What are Degenerate Orbitals?
Degenerate orbitals are orbitals that have the same energy level. In a hydrogen-like atom, all orbitals with the same principal quantum number (n) are degenerate.
Formula for Number of Orbitals:
The number of orbitals in a given energy level n is given by:
Number of orbitals = n²

This is because for each n, there are n subshells (l = 0 to n-1), and each subshell has (2l+1) orbitals.

For n = 4:
Subshell (l) Orbitals (2l+1) Count
s (l=0) 2(0)+1 = 1 1 orbital
p (l=1) 2(1)+1 = 3 3 orbitals
d (l=2) 2(2)+1 = 5 5 orbitals
f (l=3) 2(3)+1 = 7 7 orbitals
TOTAL 1 + 3 + 5 + 7 = 16 orbitals

All 16 orbitals in the n=4 level are degenerate (same energy) in a hydrogen atom.

✓ Final Answer: There are 16 degenerate orbitals in energy level n=4.

Question.content: Express the wave and particle view of radiant energy.

Answer.content: Wave view: Electromagnetic waves with wavelength and frequency. Particle view: Photons/quanta with energy E = hν.

Explanation.content:
Wave-Particle Duality of Radiant Energy

The Dual Nature of Light:
Radiant energy (light) exhibits both wave-like and particle-like properties - this is called wave-particle duality.
1. Wave View of Radiant Energy:
  • Light behaves as electromagnetic waves
  • Characterized by wavelength (λ) and frequency (ν)
  • Relationship: \(c = \lambda\nu\) where c is the speed of light
  • Exhibits wave phenomena like diffraction, interference, and polarization
  • Demonstrated by Young's double-slit experiment
\(c = \lambda\nu\) (wave equation)
2. Particle View of Radiant Energy:
  • Light behaves as discrete packets of energy called photons or quanta
  • Each photon carries a specific amount of energy
  • Energy is proportional to frequency (Planck's relation)
  • Exhibits particle phenomena like the photoelectric effect and Compton scattering
  • Demonstrated by Einstein's explanation of the photoelectric effect
\(E = h\nu = \frac{hc}{\lambda}\) (Planck's relation)

where h = Planck's constant (\(6.626 \times 10^{-34}\) J·s)

de Broglie's Contribution:
Louis de Broglie proposed that this duality applies to all matter, not just light:
\(\lambda = \frac{h}{p}\) where p is momentum
✓ Final Answer: Wave view: \(c = \lambda\nu\). Particle view: \(E = h\nu\).

Question.content: Planck's quantum of energy and wavelength of a photon is given by what equation?

Answer.content: E = hν = hc/λ

Explanation.content:
Planck's Quantum Relation

Max Planck's Quantum Theory:
In 1900, Max Planck proposed that energy is not continuous but is emitted or absorbed in discrete packets called quanta or photons.
The Fundamental Equation:
\(E = h\nu\)

Where:

  • E = Energy of one photon (in joules)
  • h = Planck's constant (\(6.626 \times 10^{-34}\) J·s)
  • ν = Frequency of the radiation (in s⁻¹ or Hz)
Relating Energy to Wavelength:
Since frequency and wavelength are related by \(c = \lambda\nu\), we can also write:
\(E = \frac{hc}{\lambda}\)

Where:

  • c = Speed of light (\(3.0 \times 10^8\) m/s)
  • λ = Wavelength (in meters)
Combined Form:
\(E = h\nu = \frac{hc}{\lambda}\)

This is the complete relation that connects the particle nature (energy E) with the wave nature (frequency ν and wavelength λ) of light.

✓ Final Answer: \(E = h\nu = \frac{hc}{\lambda}\)

Question.content: The property of light that defines its intensity is what?

Answer.content: Amplitude

Explanation.content:
Properties of Light

Light as a Wave:
When we treat light as a wave, it has several measurable properties:
Property What it Determines Symbol
Amplitude Intensity/Brightness A
Wavelength Color (in visible light) λ
Frequency Color/Energy ν
Velocity Speed (constant in vacuum) c
Understanding Amplitude:
  • Amplitude is the maximum displacement of a wave from its equilibrium position
  • In light waves, amplitude corresponds to the strength of the electric and magnetic fields
  • Higher amplitude = More intense (brighter) light
  • Doubling the amplitude quadruples the intensity (intensity ∝ amplitude²)
Analogy:
Think of waves on water:
  • Amplitude = height of the wave (determines how powerful the wave is)
  • Wavelength = distance between wave peaks (determines the type of wave)
✓ Final Answer: The property of light that defines its intensity is amplitude.

Question.content: A double dumbbell shape is accredited to which type of orbital?

Answer.content: d-orbital

Explanation.content:
Shapes of Atomic Orbitals

Atomic Orbital Shapes:
Different types of orbitals have characteristic shapes that come from solutions to the Schrödinger equation.
Orbital Type l value Shape Description
s-orbital l = 0 Spherical shape
p-orbital l = 1 Dumbbell shape (two lobes)
d-orbital l = 2 Double dumbbell / Cloverleaf shape (four lobes), except dz² which has a different shape
f-orbital l = 3 Complex multi-lobed shapes
The Five d-orbitals:
  • dxy, dxz, dyz: Four-lobed cloverleaf shapes oriented between axes
  • dx²-y²: Four-lobed cloverleaf shape oriented along axes
  • dz²: Dumbbell with a doughnut (toroidal ring) - looks different but is still a d-orbital
Visual Memory Aid:
  • s = sphere (simple)
  • p = peanut (one dumbbell)
  • d = double peanut (two dumbbells crossed) / four-leaf clover
✓ Final Answer: A double dumbbell shape is accredited to d-orbitals.

Question.content: According to classical physics, why did electrons not collapse into the nucleus?

Answer.content: Classical physics predicted electrons would spiral into the nucleus due to energy loss from radiation, but they don't. Quantum mechanics later explained stability through quantized energy levels.

Explanation.content:
The Atomic Stability Problem

The Classical Physics Prediction:
According to classical electromagnetic theory (Maxwell's equations):
  • An accelerating charged particle radiates energy
  • An electron orbiting a nucleus is constantly accelerating (changing direction)
  • Therefore, an orbiting electron should continuously lose energy as radiation
  • As it loses energy, it would spiral inward toward the nucleus
  • Calculations showed this collapse would happen in about 10⁻¹¹ seconds
The Problem:
Atoms are stable! Electrons do NOT collapse into the nucleus. Classical physics could not explain this.
The Solution - Quantum Mechanics:
Niels Bohr (1913) and later quantum mechanics resolved this paradox:
  • Quantized energy levels: Electrons can only exist in specific, allowed orbits with fixed energies
  • Stationary states: Electrons in these allowed orbits do NOT radiate energy, despite accelerating
  • Quantum jumps: Electrons only emit or absorb energy when jumping between allowed levels
  • Wave-particle duality: Electrons are not tiny particles orbiting like planets, but have wave-like nature described by wavefunctions
Heisenberg's Uncertainty Principle:
Also contributes: if an electron were confined to the nucleus, its momentum would be enormous - it's "uncertain" where the electron actually is.
✓ Final Answer: Classical physics couldn't explain atomic stability - it predicted electron collapse. Quantum mechanics resolved this with quantized energy levels where electrons don't radiate.

Question.content: What is one major contribution of J.J. Thompson to the development of the atomic model?

Answer.content: Discovery of the electron and proposal of the 'plum pudding' model

Explanation.content:
J.J. Thompson's Contributions

Who was J.J. Thompson?
Sir Joseph John Thompson (1856-1940) was a British physicist who revolutionized our understanding of the atom.
1. Discovery of the Electron (1897)
  • Conducted cathode ray tube experiments
  • Showed that cathode rays were composed of negatively charged particles
  • Measured the charge-to-mass ratio (e/m) of these particles
  • Discovered that these particles were much smaller than atoms
  • Called them "corpuscles" - later named "electrons"
  • This proved that atoms are divisible and contain smaller particles
Electrons are negatively charged subatomic particles
2. The "Plum Pudding" Model (1904)
  • Proposed the first model of the atom's internal structure
  • Envisioned the atom as a sphere of positive charge
  • Negatively charged electrons were embedded within like plums in a pudding
  • Also called the "blueberry muffin" model
  • Explained overall electrical neutrality of atoms
Positive sphere with embedded electrons
Legacy:
Thompson won the Nobel Prize in Physics in 1906. His work paved the way for later models by Rutherford, Bohr, and others. The plum pudding model was later disproved by Rutherford's gold foil experiment.
✓ Final Answer: J.J. Thompson discovered the electron and proposed the "plum pudding" model of the atom.

Question.content: What is one major contribution of R. Millikan to the development of the atomic model?

Answer.content: Oil drop experiment to determine the charge of the electron

Explanation.content:
Robert Millikan's Contribution

Who was Robert Millikan?
Robert Andrews Millikan (1868-1953) was an American physicist who made precise measurements of the electron's charge.
The Oil Drop Experiment (1909)

Experimental Setup:

  • Fine oil droplets were sprayed into a chamber
  • Droplets fell under gravity between two charged plates
  • X-rays were used to give the droplets a charge
  • By adjusting the voltage, droplets could be suspended motionless
  • Millikan measured the terminal velocity and balancing voltage

What He Discovered:

  • All charges on the droplets were multiples of a fundamental unit
  • This fundamental unit was the charge of a single electron
  • He calculated e = 1.592 × 10⁻¹⁹ C (very close to modern value 1.602 × 10⁻¹⁹ C)
Charge of electron (e) = \(1.602 \times 10^{-19}\) C
Why This Was Important:
  • J.J. Thompson had discovered the electron and measured its charge-to-mass ratio (e/m)
  • Millikan's measurement of e alone allowed calculation of the electron's mass
  • Electron mass = \(\frac{e}{(e/m)} = 9.1 \times 10^{-31}\) kg
  • Confirmed that electrons are indeed fundamental particles with quantized charge
  • Provided experimental proof that electric charge comes in discrete units
Recognition:
Millikan won the Nobel Prize in Physics in 1923 for this work.
✓ Final Answer: R. Millikan determined the charge of the electron through his famous oil drop experiment.

Question.content: According to valence bond theory, what is the hybridization type and structure of ethane?

Answer.content: Hybridization: sp³. Structure: Tetrahedral around each carbon.

Explanation.content:
Ethane (C₂H₆) - Hybridization and Structure

What is Ethane?
Ethane is the simplest hydrocarbon containing a carbon-carbon single bond, with molecular formula C₂H₆.
Step 1: Determine the hybridization

Each carbon atom in ethane is bonded to:

  • 3 hydrogen atoms
  • 1 other carbon atom

Total bonds per carbon = 4 sigma (σ) bonds

For 4 sigma bonds, carbon must use sp³ hybridization:

  • One s orbital + three p orbitals hybridize
  • Forms four equivalent sp³ hybrid orbitals
  • Each sp³ orbital has 25% s-character and 75% p-character
Hybridization: sp³
Step 2: Determine the structure

sp³ hybrid orbitals arrange themselves to minimize electron pair repulsion:

  • They point toward the corners of a tetrahedron
  • Bond angle = 109.5°
  • Each carbon is at the center of a tetrahedron
  • The H-C-H and H-C-C bond angles are all approximately 109.5°
Structure: Tetrahedral around each carbon
Bond Formation:
  • C-C bond: Overlap of one sp³ orbital from each carbon (sigma bond)
  • C-H bonds: Overlap of carbon sp³ orbitals with hydrogen 1s orbitals
  • All bonds in ethane are single (sigma) bonds
✓ Final Answer: Ethane has sp³ hybridization and tetrahedral structure around each carbon atom.

Question.content: Using the American Chemical Society classification, Scandium (Sc) belongs to which group and which period?

Answer.content: Group: 3, Period: 4

Explanation.content:
Scandium (Sc) - Position in Periodic Table

About Scandium:
Scandium is a chemical element with symbol Sc and atomic number 21. It is a silvery-white metallic d-block element.
Electronic Configuration:
Sc: \(1s^2 2s^2 2p^6 3s^2 3p^6 4s^2 3d^1\)
or [Ar] \(4s^2 3d^1\)
Determining Group and Period:

Period: The highest principal quantum number (n) containing electrons is 4 (from 4s²), so Scandium is in Period 4.

Group: In the American Chemical Society (ACS) / IUPAC system:

  • Scandium is the first element in the transition series
  • It has 3 valence electrons (2 in 4s + 1 in 3d)
  • It belongs to Group 3
  • Group 3 also includes Yttrium (Y), Lanthanum (La), and Actinium (Ac)
Group 3, Period 4
Properties of Scandium:
  • It is the first transition metal in the fourth period
  • Exhibits +3 oxidation state almost exclusively
  • Compounds are generally colorless (no d-d transitions due to d⁰ configuration in Sc³⁺)
  • Used in aluminum alloys for aerospace components
✓ Final Answer: Scandium (Sc) belongs to Group 3 and Period 4.

Question.content: What is the bond type in calcium chloride (CaCl₂)?

Answer.content: Ionic

Explanation.content:
Bond Type in Calcium Chloride (CaCl₂)

What is Calcium Chloride?
Calcium chloride is an inorganic compound with the formula CaCl₂. It is a white crystalline solid at room temperature and highly soluble in water.
Step 1: Identify the elements
  • Calcium (Ca): Group 2 (alkaline earth metal)
  • Chlorine (Cl): Group 17 (halogen, non-metal)

Metal + Non-metal generally indicates ionic bonding.

Step 2: Electron transfer

Calcium (atomic number 20): [Ar] 4s²

  • Has 2 valence electrons
  • Low ionization energy - easily loses 2 electrons
  • Forms Ca²⁺ ion with stable argon configuration

Chlorine (atomic number 17): [Ne] 3s² 3p⁵

  • Has 7 valence electrons
  • High electron affinity - easily gains 1 electron
  • Forms Cl⁻ ion with stable argon configuration
\(Ca \rightarrow Ca^{2+} + 2e^-\)
\(2Cl + 2e^- \rightarrow 2Cl^-\)
Step 3: Ionic bond formation

The electrostatic attraction between:

  • Positively charged calcium ions (Ca²⁺)
  • Negatively charged chloride ions (Cl⁻)

Results in the formation of an ionic bond.

\(Ca^{2+} + 2Cl^- \rightarrow CaCl_2\)
Evidence of Ionic Character:
  • High melting point (772°C)
  • Conducts electricity when molten or in aqueous solution
  • Soluble in polar solvents like water
  • Forms a crystalline lattice structure
✓ Final Answer: Calcium chloride (CaCl₂) has ionic bonding.

Question.content: Write the complete electronic configuration of Bromine (Br).

Answer.content: 1s² 2s² 2p⁶ 3s² 3p⁶ 4s² 3d¹⁰ 4p⁵

Explanation.content:
Electronic Configuration of Bromine (Br)

About Bromine:
Bromine is a chemical element with symbol Br and atomic number 35. It is a reddish-brown liquid halogen at room temperature.
Step-by-Step Filling (Aufbau Principle):
Order Subshell Electrons Added Running Total
11s²22
22s²24
32p⁶610
43s²212
53p⁶618
64s²220
73d¹⁰1030
84p⁵535
Complete Electronic Configuration:
\(1s^2 2s^2 2p^6 3s^2 3p^6 4s^2 3d^{10} 4p^5\)

Noble Gas Notation (shorter form):

[Ar] \(4s^2 3d^{10} 4p^5\)

where [Ar] represents the configuration of argon: \(1s^2 2s^2 2p^6 3s^2 3p^6\)

Valence Electrons:
Bromine has 7 valence electrons (2 in 4s and 5 in 4p), which is why it is in Group 17 (halogens) and typically forms a -1 oxidation state.
✓ Final Answer: \(1s^2 2s^2 2p^6 3s^2 3p^6 4s^2 3d^{10} 4p^5\)

Question.content: The observations of what two phenomena formed the basis of the wave-particle duality principle?

Answer.content: Photoelectric effect (particle nature) and Diffraction/Interference (wave nature)

Explanation.content:
Wave-Particle Duality

What is Wave-Particle Duality?
Wave-particle duality is the concept that every quantum entity (like light and electrons) exhibits both wave-like and particle-like properties, depending on how it is observed.
1. Evidence for Particle Nature - The Photoelectric Effect

Experiment: When light shines on a metal surface, electrons are emitted.

Key Observations (Einstein, 1905):

  • Electrons are emitted only if light frequency exceeds a threshold
  • Increasing light intensity does NOT increase electron energy, only number
  • There is no time lag - emission is instantaneous

Conclusion: Light must consist of discrete packets of energy called photons (particle nature). Energy of each photon = hν.

\(E = h\nu\) (Planck-Einstein relation)
2. Evidence for Wave Nature - Diffraction and Interference

Experiment: Young's double-slit experiment (1801)

Key Observations:

  • Light passing through two narrow slits creates an interference pattern
  • Alternating bright and dark bands appear on a screen
  • This pattern can only be explained by waves interfering constructively and destructively
  • Particles would create only two bright bands, not multiple bands

Conclusion: Light must behave as waves to produce interference patterns.

\(c = \lambda\nu\) (wave equation)
de Broglie's Synthesis (1924):
Louis de Broglie proposed that if light (waves) can behave as particles, then particles (like electrons) should also behave as waves:
\(\lambda = \frac{h}{p}\) (de Broglie wavelength)

This was later confirmed experimentally by electron diffraction.

✓ Final Answer: Wave-particle duality is based on the photoelectric effect (particle nature) and diffraction/interference (wave nature).

Question.content: The 6th period of the modern periodic table contains how many elements?

Explanation.content:
The 6th Period of the Periodic Table

Overview of Periods:
Periods are the horizontal rows in the periodic table. The length of each period varies based on how many electrons can fill the subshells.
Period Lengths:
Period Elements Number of Elements
1H - He2
2Li - Ne8
3Na - Ar8
4K - Kr18
5Rb - Xe18
6Cs - Rn32
7Fr - Og32 (incomplete)
Why 32 Elements in Period 6?

Period 6 includes the filling of:

  • 6s subshell: 2 elements (Cs, Ba)
  • 4f subshell (lanthanides): 14 elements (La/Ce to Lu)
  • 5d subshell: 10 elements (La to Hg, including Hf)
  • 6p subshell: 6 elements (Tl to Rn)

Total = 2 + 14 + 10 + 6 = 32 elements

Period 6 starts with Cesium (Cs, atomic number 55) and ends with Radon (Rn, atomic number 86).

Cs (55) through Rn (86) = 86 - 55 + 1 = 32 elements
Note:
Period 6 includes the lanthanide series (14 elements from Cerium to Lutetium), which are usually placed below the main table to keep it compact.
✓ Final Answer: The 6th period contains 32 elements.

Question.content: What formed the basis of the periodic law?

Answer.content: Atomic number (modern) or atomic mass (Mendeleev's original)

Explanation.content:
The Periodic Law

What is the Periodic Law?
The periodic law states that the physical and chemical properties of elements are periodic functions of their atomic numbers (modern version).
1. Mendeleev's Original Periodic Law (1869)

Dmitri Mendeleev arranged elements in order of increasing atomic mass and noticed that properties repeated periodically.

  • Based on atomic mass
  • Left gaps for undiscovered elements
  • Predicted properties of missing elements (e.g., gallium, germanium)
  • Had to place some elements out of mass order to keep similar properties together (e.g., tellurium and iodine)
Properties are a periodic function of atomic mass (Mendeleev)
2. Modern Periodic Law (Moseley, 1913)

Henry Moseley used X-ray spectroscopy to determine the atomic number (nuclear charge) of elements.

  • Found that frequency of X-rays emitted correlated with atomic number, not atomic mass
  • Proposed that elements should be arranged by atomic number
  • Resolved the tellurium-iodine anomaly (Te has higher mass but lower atomic number than I)
Properties are a periodic function of atomic number (Modern)
Why Atomic Number is Fundamental:
  • Atomic number = number of protons = nuclear charge
  • Electron configuration (which determines chemical properties) depends on nuclear charge
  • Atomic mass can vary with isotopes, but atomic number is invariant
✓ Final Answer: The periodic law is based on atomic number (modern) or atomic mass (Mendeleev's original).

Question.content: Group two elements will burn in air to form what type of compounds?

Answer.content: Metal oxides (MO)

Explanation.content:
Reaction of Group II Elements with Air

Group II Elements (Alkaline Earth Metals):
Beryllium (Be), Magnesium (Mg), Calcium (Ca), Strontium (Sr), Barium (Ba), Radium (Ra)
Reaction with Oxygen:

When heated in air (which contains about 21% oxygen), group II metals burn vigorously to form metal oxides:

\(2M + O_2 \rightarrow 2MO\)

Where M represents the group II metal and MO is the metal oxide.

Examples:

  • Magnesium: \(2Mg + O_2 \rightarrow 2MgO\) (magnesium oxide - white powder)
  • Calcium: \(2Ca + O_2 \rightarrow 2CaO\) (calcium oxide - quicklime)
  • Barium: \(2Ba + O_2 \rightarrow 2BaO\) (barium oxide)
Note about Beryllium:
Beryllium is an exception - it forms a thin, protective oxide layer (BeO) that prevents further reaction, similar to aluminum.
✓ Final Answer: Group two elements burn in air to form metal oxides (MO).

Question.content: Group two elements dissolve in water to form what type of compounds?

Answer.content: Metal hydroxides M(OH)₂

Explanation.content:
Group II Elements Reacting with Water

The Reaction:
Group II metals (or their oxides) react with water to form metal hydroxides. These are alkaline (basic) solutions, which is why they're called "alkaline earth metals".
Case 1: Metals reacting directly with water
\(M + 2H_2O \rightarrow M(OH)_2 + H_2\)

Example with calcium:

\(Ca + 2H_2O \rightarrow Ca(OH)_2 + H_2\)

Calcium hydroxide (limewater) is produced along with hydrogen gas.

Case 2: Metal oxides reacting with water
\(MO + H_2O \rightarrow M(OH)_2\)

Example with calcium oxide (quicklime):

\(CaO + H_2O \rightarrow Ca(OH)_2\)

This reaction is called "slaking of lime" and produces a lot of heat.

Trend in Reactivity:
Reactivity with water increases down the group:
  • Be: No reaction with water (protected by oxide layer)
  • Mg: Reacts slowly with hot water/steam
  • Ca, Sr, Ba: React vigorously even with cold water
✓ Final Answer: Group two elements dissolve in water to form metal hydroxides M(OH)₂.

Question.content: Permanent hardness of water can be caused by what two types of compounds?

Answer.content: Chlorides (CaCl₂, MgCl₂) and sulfates (CaSO₄, MgSO₄) of calcium and magnesium

Explanation.content:
Causes of Permanent Hardness

What is Permanent Hardness?
Permanent hardness is a type of water hardness that cannot be removed by simply boiling the water. It is caused by dissolved minerals that remain in water even when heated.
The Main Causes:

Permanent hardness is caused by the presence of:

Compound Type Examples Formula
Chlorides Calcium chloride
Magnesium chloride		 CaCl₂
MgCl₂
Sulfates Calcium sulfate
Magnesium sulfate		 CaSO₄ (gypsum)
MgSO₄ (Epsom salt)
Why These Cause Hardness:
These compounds dissolve in water to form Ca²⁺ and Mg²⁺ ions. These ions react with soap to form insoluble scum, preventing lathering and causing the "hard" water effect.
Difference from Temporary Hardness:
Temporary hardness is caused by calcium and magnesium bicarbonates Ca(HCO₃)₂ and Mg(HCO₃)₂, which decompose when boiled. Permanent hardness compounds do not decompose on heating.
✓ Final Answer: Permanent hardness is caused by chlorides (CaCl₂, MgCl₂) and sulfates (CaSO₄, MgSO₄) of calcium and magnesium.

Question.content: Permanent hardness of water can be removed by what method?

Answer.content: Adding washing soda (sodium carbonate)

Explanation.content:
Removing Permanent Hardness

Why Boiling Doesn't Work:
Unlike temporary hardness (caused by bicarbonates), permanent hardness compounds (chlorides and sulfates) do not decompose when boiled. So boiling alone will not soften permanently hard water.
The Washing Soda Method:

Adding washing soda (sodium carbonate, Na₂CO₃) removes permanent hardness by precipitation. The carbonate ions react with calcium and magnesium ions to form insoluble carbonates that precipitate out.

For calcium sulfate:

\(CaSO_4 + Na_2CO_3 \rightarrow CaCO_3\downarrow + Na_2SO_4\)

For calcium chloride:

\(CaCl_2 + Na_2CO_3 \rightarrow CaCO_3\downarrow + 2NaCl\)

For magnesium sulfate:

\(MgSO_4 + Na_2CO_3 \rightarrow MgCO_3\downarrow + Na_2SO_4\)

The calcium carbonate (CaCO₃) and magnesium carbonate (MgCO₃) precipitates can be removed by filtration or settling.

Other Methods to Remove Permanent Hardness:
  • Ion exchange method: Using zeolites or ion exchange resins that swap Ca²⁺ and Mg²⁺ for Na⁺ or H⁺
  • Distillation: Boiling water and collecting the steam leaves dissolved solids behind
  • Reverse osmosis: Forcing water through a semi-permeable membrane
✓ Final Answer: Permanent hardness can be removed by adding washing soda (sodium carbonate, Na₂CO₃).

Question.content: predict the existence of Li, Be2 molecular orbital theory

Answer.content: Li₂ exists (bond order 1), Be₂ does not exist (bond order 0)

Explanation.content:
Predicting Existence of Li and Be₂ Using MO Theory

Molecular Orbital Theory Basics:
MO theory predicts whether a molecule can exist by calculating its bond order:
Bond Order = ½ (Number of bonding e⁻ – Number of antibonding e⁻)
  • Bond order > 0 → molecule exists (stable)
  • Bond order = 0 → molecule does not exist (unstable)
  • Higher bond order means stronger, more stable bond
1. Lithium Molecule (Li₂)

Atomic configuration: Li (Z=3) = 1s² 2s¹

Molecular orbital diagram for Li₂: Only valence electrons (2s) participate in bonding. The two 2s orbitals combine to form:

  • σ2s (bonding orbital) - lower energy
  • σ*2s (antibonding orbital) - higher energy

Electron configuration of Li₂: Two valence electrons (one from each Li atom) fill the lower energy bonding orbital:

Li₂ = (σ2s)²

Bond order calculation:

  • Bonding electrons = 2
  • Antibonding electrons = 0
Bond Order = (2 - 0)/2 = 1

Conclusion: Bond order of 1 means Li₂ has a stable single bond. Li₂ molecules do exist in lithium vapor.

2. Beryllium Molecule (Be₂)

Atomic configuration: Be (Z=4) = 1s² 2s²

Molecular orbital diagram for Be₂: Four valence electrons (two from each Be atom) fill the molecular orbitals:

  • First two electrons fill σ2s (bonding)
  • Next two electrons fill σ*2s (antibonding)

Electron configuration of Be₂:

Be₂ = (σ2s)² (σ*2s)²

Bond order calculation:

  • Bonding electrons = 2 (in σ2s)
  • Antibonding electrons = 2 (in σ*2s)
Bond Order = (2 - 2)/2 = 0

Conclusion: Bond order of 0 means there is no net bonding interaction. Be₂ does not exist as a stable molecule.

Note: The 1s core electrons (which would give σ1s² σ*1s²) cancel each other out (bonding and antibonding both filled), so they contribute nothing to the bond order.
✓ Final Prediction: Li₂ exists (bond order 1). Be₂ does not exist (bond order 0).

Question.content: Arrange the following atoms in the order of increasing electronegativity: Ba, Na, K, C, S, N, F

Answer.content: Ba < K < Na < C < S < N < F

Explanation.content:
Electronegativity Order

Electronegativity Trend:
Electronegativity increases from left to right across a period and decreases down a group.
Element Group Period Electronegativity
Ba260.89
K140.82
Na130.93
C1422.55
S1632.58
N1523.04
F1723.98
Ba < K < Na < C < S < N < F
✓ Final Answer: Ba < K < Na < C < S < N < F

Question.content: Calculate the wavelength of a radiation whose frequency is \(1.8 \times 10^3 \, \text{s}^{-1}\). (Given: velocity of light \(c = 3.0 \times 10^8 \, \text{m/s}\))

Explanation.content:
Wavelength Calculation

Step 1: Recall the wave equation
\(c = \lambda\nu\) where c = speed of light, λ = wavelength, ν = frequency
Step 2: Rearrange to solve for wavelength
\(\lambda = \frac{c}{\nu}\)
Step 3: Substitute values
\(\lambda = \frac{3.0 \times 10^8 \, \text{m/s}}{1.8 \times 10^3 \, \text{s}^{-1}}\)
Step 4: Calculate
\(\frac{3.0}{1.8} = 1.666...\) and \(\frac{10^8}{10^3} = 10^5\)
\(\lambda = 1.67 \times 10^5 \, \text{m}\)
✓ Final Answer: \(1.67 \times 10^5 \, \text{m}\)

Question.content: Give all possible values for Azimuthal quantum number (l) and Magnetic quantum number (m_l) for principal shell n = 2.

Explanation.content:
Quantum Numbers for n=2

Azimuthal Quantum Number (l):
l can have values from 0 to (n-1). For n=2:
  • l = 0 (s-subshell)
  • l = 1 (p-subshell)
Magnetic Quantum Number (m_l):
m_l can have values from -l to +l, including zero.

For l = 0: m_l = 0 (one value - 2s orbital)

For l = 1: m_l = -1, 0, +1 (three values - 2p orbitals)

Summary Table:
n l m_l Subshell
2002s
21-1, 0, +12p
✓ Final Answer: l = 0, 1. For l=0, m_l=0. For l=1, m_l=-1, 0, +1.

Question.content: Using appropriate equations only, illustrate the metallic and non-metallic character of Boron.

Answer.content: Metallic character: 2B + 3Cl₂ → 2BCl₃. Non-metallic character: 4B + 3O₂ → 2B₂O₃; B₂O₃ + 3H₂O → 2H₃BO₃

Explanation.content:
Metallic and Non-metallic Character of Boron

Boron is a Metalloid:
It exhibits both metallic and non-metallic properties.
Metallic Character:
Like metals, boron reacts with halogens to form halides:
\(2B + 3Cl_2 \rightarrow 2BCl_3\)

Note: BCl₃ is covalent, showing boron's incomplete metallic character.

Non-metallic Character:
Like non-metals, boron forms an acidic oxide that reacts with water to give an acid:
\(4B + 3O_2 \rightarrow 2B_2O_3\)
\(B_2O_3 + 3H_2O \rightarrow 2H_3BO_3\) (boric acid)
✓ Final Answer: \(2B + 3Cl_2 \rightarrow 2BCl_3\), \(4B + 3O_2 \rightarrow 2B_2O_3\), \(B_2O_3 + 3H_2O \rightarrow 2H_3BO_3\)

Question.content: List the four types of stable binary interhalogen compounds with a specific example for each.

Answer.content: 1. XY type: ClF. 2. XY₃ type: ClF₃. 3. XY₅ type: BrF₅. 4. XY₇ type: IF₇.

Explanation.content:
Interhalogen Compounds

What are Interhalogen Compounds?
Compounds formed between two different halogen atoms, with general formula XYₙ where n is odd.
Type Formula Example Name
1XYClFChlorine monofluoride
2XY₃ClF₃Chlorine trifluoride
3XY₅BrF₅Bromine pentafluoride
4XY₇IF₇Iodine heptafluoride
✓ Final Answer: XY (ClF), XY₃ (ClF₃), XY₅ (BrF₅), XY₇ (IF₇)

Question.content: Give reasons why Beryllium shows some unusual properties from other elements in its group.

Answer.content: 1. Very small size and high charge density. 2. High ionization energy. 3. High electronegativity. 4. Lack of d-orbitals. 5. Forms covalent compounds. 6. Amphoteric hydroxide.

Explanation.content:
Why Beryllium is Different

  • Very small size and high charge density: Leads to high polarizing power
  • High ionization energy: Much higher than other Group II elements
  • High electronegativity: About 1.57, while others are 0.9-1.0
  • Lack of d-orbitals: Cannot expand coordination number beyond 4
  • Forms covalent compounds: Others form ionic compounds
  • Amphoteric hydroxide: Be(OH)₂ dissolves in both acids and bases; others are basic only
  • Diagonal relationship with Al: Shows similarity with aluminum
✓ Final Answer: Small size, high charge density, high ionization energy, high electronegativity, lack of d-orbitals, covalent bonding, amphoteric hydroxide.

Question.content: What is the trend of electronegativity and ionization energies for groups one and two?

Answer.content: Both electronegativity and ionization energy decrease down the group for Group I and Group II elements.

Explanation.content:
Trends in Groups I and II

Ionization Energy:
  • Decreases down both groups
  • Reason: Atomic size increases, outer electrons are farther from nucleus and more shielded
  • Group I example: Li > Na > K > Rb > Cs
Electronegativity:
  • Decreases down both groups
  • Reason: Increasing atomic size means nucleus has less pull on bonding electrons
  • Group I example: Li > Na > K > Rb > Cs
  • Group II example: Be > Mg > Ca > Sr > Ba
Both properties DECREASE down the group
✓ Final Answer: Both electronegativity and ionization energy decrease down Group I and Group II.
